content:
To find the minimum value of $b$ across all "pupusa" pairs, we first need to understand the conditions given.

The problem states that we need to find a pair $(a,b)$ such that:
1. $a$ is a positive integer.
2. $b$ is a positive integer.
3. $a < 391$.
4. The least common multiple (LCM) of $a$ and $b$ is greater than the LCM of $a$ and 391.

Let's break it down step by step.

Since $\text{lcm}(a,b) > \text{lcm}(a,391)$, it means $b$ must have a prime factor greater than or equal to any of the prime factors of 391. 

To find the prime factors of 391, let's use prime factorization.

391 can be factorized into $17 \times 23$.

Now, we know that the prime factors of $a$ can include $2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13,$ or any prime less than 17, since $a < 391$. However, in order to get the least $b$ we must introduce new prime factors into the product. 

Looking at the prime factorization of multiples of 391 up to 391 (as $a$ has to be less than 391) we can identify what primes (including the new ones we introduced into our product) have been added (the new primes here being 2, 3, 5, etc.), to give us the least $b$ that could contain a higher lcm.

391 has prime factors $17 \times 23$ so we can only introduce a prime higher than $17$ as $391$ can't have $2$ or $3$ added into the LCM to compare to $\text{lcm}(a,391)$

This means that  the minimum possible pupusa will have an $a$ that contains all the lower primes and at least one of the higher primes (in our case at least one prime higher than 17, but in order to use less than 391 we must only be able to choose 19).

This means that 19 is the least possible prime for the pupusa other than 391 to have and $a$ must have prime factors $19$ but less than $23$.

For the minimum $b$ we want to take $a = \text{lcm}(1,2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19) = \text{lcm}(1) *   \text{lcm}(2) * \text{lcm}(3) *  \text{lcm}(5) * \text{lcm}(7) *\text{lcm}(11) *  \text{lcm}(13) * \text{lcm}(17) * 19 = 19$.

Since $\text{lcm}(19,391)= 391\infty$, it does seem plausible  and $a = \text{lcm}(1,2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19)$ is also plausible in this context as $\text{lcm}(19,391)=\text{lcm}(19,23) = \text{lcm}(391,19)= 391\infty$, so it follows $a$ equals $\text{lcm}(1,2,3,5,7,11,13,19) = \text{lcm}(1) * \text{lcm}(19) * \text{lcm}(2) *  \text{lcm}(3) *  \text{lcm}(5) * \text{lcm}(7) * \text{lcm}(11) * \text{lcm}(13) $. 

However this is still a bit too much so $a$ should be a value of $\text{lcm}(1,2,3,5,7,11,19)$ = 3230 because that gives $\text{lcm}(3230,391)= 3230\infty$.

Therefore the minimum possible value of $b$ is $\boxed{3230 \times 23 = 74110}$

To find the prime factors of 391, let's use prime factorization.

391 can be factorized into $17 \times 23$.

Now, we know that the prime factors of $a$ can include $2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13,$ or any prime less than 17, since $a < 391$. However, in order to get the least $b$ we must introduce new prime factors into the product. 

Looking at the prime factorization of multiples of 391 up to 391 (as $a$ has to be less than 391) we can identify what primes (including the new ones we introduced into our product) have been added (the new primes here being 2, 3, 5, etc.), to give us the least $b$ that could contain a higher lcm.

This means that  the minimum possible pupusa will have an $a$ that contains all the lower primes and at least one of the higher primes (in our case at least one prime higher than 17, but in order to use less than 391 we must only be able to choose 19).
